meta.title: Video by jinabaobina
meta.description:
Michelin Bib Gourmand duck in LA
I kept seeing this place all over my feed and finally got to try it. I’m so glad I ordered multiple dishes because I’ve never had duck prepared in so many different ways (only had Peking duck🤣) 

My absolute favorite was the smoked duck. The depth of flavors and balance was DELICIOUS. If I came back with family, I’d definitely order the clay pot roasted duck. It feels lighter and healthier, and I know they would love it. 
But if I’m coming back just for myself, it’s 1000% for the smoked duck.

Pricing and details below:

📍 Dha Rae Ok 1106 S Western Ave Los Angeles, CA 90006 Koreatown

PRICE LIST
Roasted Duck $130 (Pre order at least 1 day in advance) stuffed with sweet potatoes, seeds, herbs
Smoked Duck $125 Sliced Duck $46 Chopped Duck $46

Kimchi fried rice and spicy duck noodle soup are included with the duck dishes and cannot be ordered separately.

🅿️ Parking lot available in the back!

#koreanfood #losangeles #lafoodie

transcript.transcript: This is the only place in the U.S. that specializes in duck like this. We tried four different duck dishes and I saved my favorite for last. First is the viral hold duck roasted in a clay pot. It's roasted for four hours and stuff with herbs. A variety of seeds, sticky rice and potatoes. Just look at this. The moment I tasted it, my first thought was, I wish my mom and grandma could taste this. I don't even know what the name of the seed is, but my grandma loves it. And this is the only restaurant I've ever seen use it. You also get these spicy noodles cooked with the duck bones. Tip, you do need to pre-order this at least one day in advance. Next is a sliced duck. I loved how flavorful it was on its own. Third is the lightly marinated chopped duck. My only rule here is patience. Wait for it to turn this color, the texture, the flavor, per faction. And the best part is if you order the sliced duck or the chopped duck, you get this kimchi fried rice that's cooked in the duck fat. Last is the smoked duck, which was my favorite. If you know me, you know how much I love blending flavors and this dish is all about that. Smoke duck dipped in the sauce's top with onions, all wrapped in pickled radish is so good. I love this experience, especially trying something completely new with my friends. I can see why Tadeok or Nameshelin bibgormond. I'll add more details in pricing in the caption. Would you try Tadeok?

meta.title: Video by jinabaobina
meta.description:
Chicago staple for Koreans since the 1980s.
Growing up in Chicago, there were not many places we went out to eat, and this was one of my family’s go to spots. I had not been back in over a decade, and I love that it still feels exactly the same. The interior, the vibe, the memories.

Honestly, after all these years, I have only ever ordered two things here. If you are in Chicago and craving jajangmyeon, this is a great place to try.

PRICE LIST
Sweet and sour pork $17 
Jajangmyeun $11

Peking Mandarin Restaurant in Albany Park
3459 W Lawrence Ave
Chicago, IL 60625

#chicagofood #koreanfood #chicago

transcript.transcript: Every Korean in Chicago knows this is a place to get jajangyeon. It's a Chicago staple that's been open since the 1980s, and my family has been coming here for over 30 years. This is Peking Mandarin Restaurant near Albany Park, a Korean Chinese restaurant specializing in jajangyeon and jjampung. I hadn't eaten here in over 10 years, and I could not believe how everything still looked exactly the same. The interior, the decorations, even the zodiac placemats, nothing has changed, true to its origin. I've only tried two things here. Jajangyeon, also known as black bee noodles and the tongue, or crispy fried pork with the sweet and sour sauce that's commonly ordered with the noodles. The jajangyeon tasted exactly how I remember it. They're flavorful and full of big chunks of pork and vegetables. The sweet and sour pork had the perfect balance of meat to breading. Eating the noodles with the pork and the yellow radish together is a classic method and it hits every time. This was one of my favorite meals growing up. My family rarely ate out because it was expensive, so we mostly ate homemade Korean food, which honestly can't beat that, but it made going to a restaurant feel special. And this was one of our go-tos. It's not LA or soul-level good, but considering the limited Korean options in jjajangyeon, this one is top-notch. So many of my childhood memories live here. Walking back in felt like stepping into the past, and I hope more people get to experience this place. Would you try Peking Mandarin?

meta.title: Video by jinabaobina
meta.description:
I would fly back to New York just for this

I already love Chinese food, but this spot made me love it even more. We ordered so much off the menu, and honestly… I’d get almost everything again. Next time, I’d probably reorder all of these and just add new dishes on top.

I wish I had more footage because this meal deserved a longer video, but here’s everything I need to say about my favorites:

📍 Antidote in Brooklyn (I think they also have a second location) @antidote__nyc 

Scallion pancake $12: So flaky and perfectly crisp

Dan dan noodles $14: Chewy noodles with the perfect balance of spice and sesame

Preserved egg with tofu $15: I was hesitant at first, but this ended up being one of my favorites. The flavor and spice were so good.

Sliced pork with garlic sauce $17: I can’t believe I didn’t include this in the video… this was actually my favorite dish 

Sichuan cold noodles $12: Super flavorful with that rich peanut sauce

I miss this place so much. I wasn’t planning a trip back to New York anytime soon… but honestly, I would go back just for Antidote.

#newyorkfood #chinesefood #brooklyn #brooklynfood #newyork

transcript.transcript: This restaurant in New York was so good I forgot to keep filming once I started eating. It's located in Brooklyn. My friends who live here told me it's one of their favorite spots for Chinese food so I had to try it and they were right. This ended up being my favorite place I ate during my entire trip. The only downside is now that I'm back in LA I can't have this whenever I want. We ordered so much and honestly I'd get everything again. My favorites were the Dandan noodles, preserved egg with tofu, Sichuan cold noodles, wontons and chili oil, and the scallion pancake. The vibe is so nice and the prices are very fair. I genuinely hope more people get to try this place because it made me love Chinese food even more than I already did. Would you try antidote?

meta.title: Video by jinabaobina
meta.description:
Michelin Guide 5 years in a row?!
I didn’t get to mention everything we ordered in the video, so here’s the full breakdown.

📍 Soothr @soothr_nyc 

Everything we ordered:

Yum Kaw Moo (pork salad) $16�6.5/10 very lime forward and tangy, good but not super memorable

Eggplant buns $14�7.8/10 love eggplant and this was a solid version, would order again

Shrimp Pad Thai $21�6/10 pretty basic, I’ve definitely had better

Nam-Tok Moo $20�surprisingly really good, especially since it was my first time trying pork blood soup

Koong Karee�6.5/10 we expected to love this more based on reviews, but it felt like a standard shrimp curry

Mango Sundae $15�6/10 couldn’t really taste mango 

Mii Moo Tod $25�9.5/10 my favorite. insane crunch over dry vermicelli noodles, soaked up all the flavor from the garlic and shallots. simple but so good. this alone would bring me back

Chicken Khao Soi $24�7.5/10 their most popular dish. broth was rich and flavorful, noodles were cooked well. chicken felt slightly overdone from sitting in the broth, so the eating experience wasn’t as enjoyable, but still a very solid dish

#thaifood #newyorkfood #michelinguide #eastvillage #nyceats

transcript.transcript: This tie restaurant in New York has been featured in the Michelin Guide for five years straight, and I found a dish I could eat every day for months. It's located in East Village. This was one of the spots Keenan was most excited about on our New York trip. And for the first time, I didn't order my usual pat tie and explored other interesting tie dishes I'd never tried. I put the details of what we ordered in the caption if you want to read about all the dishes because we went with one of Keenan's best friends who came on the trip for his birthday. I have to say. The best part about eating with friends is getting to try dishes you would never order for yourself. And serendipitously, that's exactly how I found two favorites of the night. First, the Namthukmu, a spicy savory pork blood soup. I was hesitant at first, especially saying pork blood on the menu, but the flavor was so rich and unique. I had 100% order it again, but my absolute favorite, the one I'd keep coming back for, Mi Mu Tad, driver, Michelin noodles, crispy pork belly, and covered in crunchy garlic and shallots. It was so good I couldn't stop thinking about it. The crunch of the pork, the subtle spice of the noodles, I'm salivating all over again. Would you try Suther.

meta.title: Video by jinabaobina
meta.description:
OG Ktown Part 2: Korean comfort food

📍 Olympic Noodle in Koreatown LA

What I ordered (Total $54 for 3 dishes)

Sujebi: hand torn dough soup. Good but not my favorite. The ratios were off to me - Too much potato and chicken, not enough hand torn dough, which is my favorite part. Also the soup contents were so big and chunky taking away from the ease of enjoying a typically minimal dish. I will say, the dough itself had a really nice chewy texture which I enjoyed.

Steamed dumplings: Heavy on chives in the best way. The flavor really comes out when you dip it in the sauce as the soy complements the filling nicely 

Fried dumplings: I thought these would be crispy, but they were more chewy. Sometimes this happens when being fried from frozen due to the water on the surface that ends up steaming the dough instead of crisping it.

Kimchi: So good. Super flavorful, and still had that crunch. I’d come back just for this. Sometimes the really flavorful kimchis feel soggy from soaking for a really long time but somehow this one retained its texture AND all of the flavor

I do wish I tried more, but going solo, there was no way I could finish everything

For this series, I don’t want to focus too much on the negatives. I still want to be honest, but in my voiceovers I want to highlight the positives as much as I can.

At the end of the day, everyone has different tastes. If this video helps you find something you end up loving here, that’s what matters most

And just showing up to these places and supporting the history of Koreatown means a lot too

#koreanfood #losangeles #lafoodie #lafood #koreannoodles

transcript.transcript: Welcome to the OG restaurants that built Korea Town, a series honoring the spots that made Korea Town what it is today. This is where you can find Ajima serving true Korean comfort food. The kind that feels like home. It's been open since the 1990s, this is Olympic noodle. They're known for Kaguksu or Knife Cut Noodles, but when I saw that they had Sujabi, hand-torned dosu, I had to order it. It's a dish I cherished growing up, and when I always asked my mom to make when I visit home. I was told to get the mandu, so I decided to try both the fried and steamed to compare. Between the two, I would get the steamed. I'll share more details about the food in the caption. The kimchi is so good. I would honestly come back just for that. Perfectly crunchy while holding the flavor of the marinade. Places like this feel like eating a piece of Kree Town's history. Being surrounded by Koreans, served by Ajima's, it honestly felt like I was back in Korea. This isn't one of the best meals I've ever had, but I'm really glad I came. Because sometimes it's not just about the food, it's about the feeling, and this place gives you that sense of comfort. Would you try Olympic noodle?

meta.title: Video by jinabaobina
meta.description:
Best KBBQ in NYC?
I’ve never fallen in love with a banchan like this before… to the point where I’m actually trying to recreate it at home. I’m coming for you, Celtuce 😭

📍 Cote Steakhouse @cotekoreansteakhouse 
꽃 🌸

Everything we ordered:

The Butcher’s Feast $82 per person�Comes with banchan, 4 cuts of meat, 2 soups, egg soufflé, and frozen yogurt
Added Japanese wagyu +$32

SO much food. I genuinely wish I could’ve taken leftovers home.
And that was everything. We were completely full by the end.
This is one of those meals that feels like a full experience. The service is attentive and seamless without being overbearing, the space is beautiful and intimate, and the food is just… high quality across the board.

I understand why Cote Korean Steakhouse is Michelin recognized.

#kbbq #nycfood #newyork #newyorkfood #michelin

transcript.transcript: The hardest reservation we made in New York was with this K-Barbecue Spa. Keenan literally hopped on at Resi at 7am on the dock a month before and we still only managed a 930 pm vlog. I went in fully expecting it to be overrated but this ended up being one of the best K-Barbecue experiences I've ever had. The space itself sets the tone. Each table feels like your own private booth. Intimate, dimly lit, and elevated without feeling too formal. We ordered the Butcher's feast, $82 per person, and added the Japanese Wagi for another 32. Before I get into anything else I need to apologize to the staff because I asked for one of the Pantan to be refilled at least 7 times. It's called Celtis. A Chinese vegetable often referred to as Chinese lettuce and it was insanely good. I need to try to make this soon. The eggsufle came out soft and airy. Almost custardy, the kimchi stew was rich and deeply fermented with that perfect balances by Centang that warms you instantly. The soybean paste stew was earthy and comforting, and then the meats. Perfectly marbled cooked right in front of you. Each bite was buttery, tender, and packed with flavor. And what really stood out wasn't just the quality, but how everything came together. The Pantan, the service, the pacing, the attention to detail. It felt like a full experience, not just the meal. Also I didn't realize this until I saw the logo, but it's not pronounced Cote. It's like goat and Korean, meaning flour. For the price, I truly think it's worth it. It's one of those places you go for the experience, the quality and the feeling of it all. I would absolutely come back, definitely worth the hype. Would you go to goat steakhouse?

meta.title: Video by jinabaobina
meta.description:
Fine dining Korean fried chicken in NYC

This spot was such a fun experience. The service, the vibe, the attention to detail… everything felt elevated. Even the chicken itself felt higher quality. Better batter, better oil, just more refined overall.

I will say, I’ve had better Korean fried chicken flavor wise. This place is less about the best chicken you’ll ever have, and more about the full experience of it all.
I’d definitely come back with friends.

📍 Coqodaq @coqodaq 

Everything I ordered: 

Black Gold Nugget $30 per nugget. It was good. You can taste the quality and the truffle, but I personally can’t justify $30 for one nugget again

Caesar Salad $19�Solid, exactly what you’d want. Well dressed and balanced

The Bucket List $46 per person. You can split all 3 flavors or customize it. We tried original, soy garlic, and gochujang. Comes with banchan, sauces, perilla seed noodles, and frozen yogurt which made it feel like a full experience

The Egg $23: I don’t drink, but this was one of my favorite parts. The lychee “egg” was so unique, almost like a sorbet texture. I’d honestly come back just for this and would 100% ask if they can make it as a mocktail

#koreanfriedchicken #newyorkfood #nyceats #koreanfood #nycfood

transcript.transcript: This is the most luxurious Korean fried chicken spot in New York. Actually, possibly in the world. For our trip, Keenan made a list of places he really wanted us to try. This was one I went into completely blind and I was shocked. Just look at this place. We started with the black gold truffle nuggets. Yes. $30 per nugget. I tried it so you don't have to. And it's good but absolutely not worth it. If you're getting a drink, the egg is actually really good. I don't drink but I honestly just ask for the leechie egg which is the ice cube shaped like a egg and made of leechie puree. Kind of like a leechie sorbet. The Caesar salad was solid. Exactly what you want. Coded perfectly. But these really unique croutons that added a nice crunch and lots of parmesan. For the main, we got the bucket list. $46 per person. We wanted to try all three flavors. Original soy garlic and guchijang. It also comes with pung chan, house made sauces, per leeched noodles and frozen yogurt. Out of all three, I like the original the most. You really get to appreciate how crispy it is. And you can switch up the flavors with the sauces. I do wish we got the duck bookie instead of the per leech. The noodles didn't really have much flavor. I would have preferred a mooning and vinegary punch to balance with the rest of the meal. Overall, the service was amazing. The space, beautiful and the quality is there. Definitely a cool spot to hit up on the weekend for dinner with friends. Would you try kukutak?
